blocks.content: He saw the first one from the porch.
Not right away — at first it was just dark settling over the street, the amber streetlights doing their job, a bat making a fast erratic circle over the Winstons' oak tree and then gone.
Then: a pulse of light in the grass below the porch steps. Small. Green-gold. There for half a second and then not, like something winking.
Another one, in the yard across the street. Three more down at the far end, over the weedy rectangle beside the Henderson property — the warmest spot on the block, best drainage after the rain.

He went through the gate and along the fence line, out to the field behind the block. It had no name, grew up to his waist in switchgrass and ragweed, chest-high in the center where a volunteer sunflower had pushed up.

Not just a few. He was standing in the edge of them — hundreds across the whole field, each light independent, each one lasting half a second before it blinked out and something else blinked on somewhere nearby.
Together they made something that wasn't quite light but felt like it. Like the field itself was breathing.
He stood there for a while.
He thought about the view from the tower. The Winstons' oak, small from sixty feet up. The town held out where you could see all of it at once.
He thought about Daisy saying yeah, it was.
He stayed out there until the first crickets started up.
